title: The Operator's Curriculum
subtitle: A learning roadmap for the two disciplines this system runs on — ICT / technical trading, and strategic AI judgment. Built for the operator, not the coder.

tracks.name: The Markets
tracks.blurb: Trading fluency, from first principles to the specific ICT logic your bot encodes. The goal isn't to trade by hand — it's to understand what your bot is deciding, and why, well enough to judge it.

modules.title: How Markets Actually Work
modules.goal: The plumbing beneath every trade: who's on the other side, and how a price is really made.
goals: Read a bid/ask spread and understand what the order book represents
Explain liquidity, market makers, and why big players need your stop-losses to fill
Distinguish spot vs. futures, and understand leverage & margin (your bot trades futures — MES, MGC, MHG — and crypto)
modules.in_your_system: Your bot's whole ICT premise is `liquidity` — sweeps, stop-hunts, buy/sell-side pools. This module is the market-microstructure reality those concepts sit on top of, and it grounds your leverage and margin exposure on the prop and futures accounts.

resources.title: The School of Pipsology
resources.note: The best jargon-free on-ramp anywhere. Early grades cover bid/ask, spreads, pips, leverage and margin from absolute zero.

resources.title: Introduction to Futures
resources.note: Authoritative on how contracts trade and clear. Pair with the Margin and The Power of Leverage companion lessons.

resources.title: Orders and the Order Book
resources.note: A liquidity provider explaining the limit order book and how orders match — straight from people who do it professionally, no upsell.

resources.title: Investopedia: Order Book & Market Maker
resources.note: Short, reliable definitions of the order book, depth, spread, and what market makers actually do. Use as a dictionary.

modules.title: Technical Analysis Fundamentals
modules.goal: The chart-reading vocabulary — candlesticks, structure, and the indicators your strategies compute.
goals: Read candlesticks, trends, and support/resistance
Understand the indicators your bot uses: VWAP, moving averages, Bollinger/squeeze, Donchian channels, RSI, MACD
Know why an indicator is a lens, not a crystal ball
modules.in_your_system: Your strategy families are named for these tools — `vwap`, `squeeze_breakout`, `trend_donchian`. Learning each indicator here tells you exactly what signal your bot is reacting to.

resources.title: StockCharts ChartSchool
resources.note: The most comprehensive free TA reference online — dedicated pages for candlesticks, support/resistance, patterns, and every indicator you listed. Your permanent reference.

resources.title: Technical Analysis Strategies for Beginners
resources.by: Investopedia · + the TA hub & VWAP pages
resources.note: Clean beginner framing plus a worked moving-average example and the 'backtest & demo before real money' discipline.

resources.title: How to Use Volume-Weighted Indicators (VWAP)
resources.note: A trustworthy broker explainer on VWAP specifically — the anchor indicator for intraday trading and one of your live strategies.

resources.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
resources.by: John J. Murphy · library/used copies common
resources.note: The canonical charting textbook most professionals learned from. The definitive deep-dive once the free hubs click.

modules.title: ICT / Smart Money Concepts
modules.goal: The heart of the whole thing. Your bot IS an ICT engine — this is its native language.
goals: Understand ICT's core thesis: price moves to seek liquidity, engineered by large players
Master the concept set your bot logs: liquidity sweeps, Fair Value Gaps, order blocks, displacement, killzones, bias
Ground every field name in the code (fvg, sweep, killzone, setup_type) in a real idea
modules.in_your_system: Your signals carry `zones[]` like {kind:"fvg",low,high} and {kind:"sweep",price}, plus meta{setup_type,killzone,bias}. This module makes every one of those readable at a glance. Start with the vocabulary before the deep setups.

resources.title: Every ICT Trading Strategy Explained in 13 Minutes
resources.note: Watch this first to grab the whole vocabulary quickly before committing to the long-form mentorship.

resources.title: The Inner Circle Trader (official)
resources.by: Michael J. Huddleston (ICT) · the entire method, free
resources.note: The authoritative primary source. The real ICT content is 100% free — treat lookalike domains and paid 'mentorships' as upsells.

resources.title: ICT Mentorship 2022 (full playlist)
resources.note: The most-recommended structured entry point — the '2022 model' is the standard beginner-to-intermediate path through the whole system.

resources.title: Key ICT Concepts
resources.note: Compact written reference covering the full concept set (FVG, order blocks, breakers, displacement) — a fast counterpart to the videos.

resources.title: ICT & SMC Key Concepts: Order Blocks & FVG
resources.note: Best for a builder: exact candle-by-candle definitions that map directly onto your bot's fvg_low/high geometry.

resources.title: Buy-Side & Sell-Side Liquidity + Sweeps
resources.by: Writ of Finance · + Aron Groups & Phidias on sweeps
resources.note: Diagrams distinguishing the liquidity pool (resting orders) from the sweep/hunt (the action your bot detects).

resources.title: ICT Killzones + Optimal Trade Entry
resources.note: The London/NY session windows your bot keys off (killzone), plus OTE — the Fib retracement 'sweet spot' after displacement.

modules.title: Your Bot's Specific Setups
modules.goal: The exact strategy families running in production — VWAP, the squeeze/Donchian breakout, and ICT scalping.
goals: Understand each setup's entry/exit logic well enough to sanity-check its trades
See how a 'squeeze' (low volatility) sets up a breakout — the coil before the move
Recognize when a setup is in or out of its ideal conditions
modules.in_your_system: These map one-to-one to your live strategies: `vwap`, `squeeze_breakout`, `trend_donchian`, `ict_scalp`. When your Strategies tab shows a win-rate per strategy, this is the 'why.'

resources.title: ICT Scalping Strategy: 4 Steps + Examples
resources.note: The Sweep → Displace → Retrace → Enter scalping sequence on 1–3m charts inside killzones — your ict_scalp.

resources.title: VWAP Strategy for Day Trading
resources.note: The breakout/pullback/trend VWAP playbook behind your vwap strategy.

resources.title: Donchian Channel Strategies: Breakout & Trend
resources.note: Donchian trend-following and the channel squeeze that precedes a volatility breakout — your trend_donchian + squeeze_breakout in one place.

resources.title: Donchian Channels: Breakout & Trend-Following
resources.note: Period selection and false-breakout filtering — the parameters that matter when tuning a systematic version.

modules.title: Risk & Trade Management
modules.goal: The math that decides whether an edge survives — position sizing, R-multiples, expectancy, drawdown.
goals: Think in R-multiples (risk units) so trades on BTC and MES compare on one axis
Compute expectancy, and see why a high win rate can still lose money (win rate vs. profit factor)
Understand position sizing, max drawdown, and the Kelly criterion (and why pros use fractional Kelly)
modules.in_your_system: Your `RiskManager.position_size()`, the per-trade refusal logic, and your dashboard's expectancy / profitFactor / maxDrawdown / R-metrics are all this module made concrete. Arguably the highest-leverage trading topic for you.

resources.title: Tharp Think — Core Trading Concepts
resources.note: Straight from the source who popularized R-multiples and expectancy — the vocabulary that makes cross-instrument trades comparable.

resources.title: Win Rate vs. Profit Factor
resources.note: Concise proof that a high win rate can still lose money, plus profit factor and drawdown benchmarks — exactly the contrast your KPIs show.

resources.title: Kelly Criterion & Maximum Drawdown
resources.note: Clear reference for the Kelly sizing formula (and why traders use fractional Kelly) and how drawdown is measured.

resources.title: Trade Your Way to Financial Freedom
resources.note: The definitive treatment of position sizing and expectancy — and why how much you bet matters more than your entry.

modules.title: Trading Psychology & Discipline
modules.goal: Why systematizing helps — and the mindset to run a bot without overriding it at the worst moment.
goals: Think in probabilities, not individual outcomes
Understand the emotional traps a systematic approach is designed to remove
Build the discipline to trust (or correctly distrust) your own system

resources.title: Trading in the Zone
resources.note: The single most-recommended trading-psychology book — thinking in probabilities and accepting risk emotionally.

resources.title: Reminiscences of a Stock Operator
resources.by: Edwin Lefèvre (on Jesse Livermore) · 1923, free full text
resources.note: A century-old classic on speculation and self-control that reads like a novel — the lessons still land.

resources.title: Chat With Traders — psychology episodes
resources.note: Real traders talking candidly about mindset, losing streaks, and discipline — the 'how it actually feels' companion to the books.

resources.title: The Disciplined Trader
resources.by: Mark Douglas · the foundational earlier work
resources.note: Building the mental discipline that any trading plan depends on. Read after Trading in the Zone if it resonates.

modules.title: A Critical Lens on ICT
modules.goal: Read this alongside Module 3, not after. Automating a method forces you to make its fuzzy calls explicit — so you must know where it's contested.
goals: Separate ICT's verifiable ideas from its inflated win-rate marketing
Understand the falsifiability problem: subjective inputs resist clean backtesting
Hold the method as one framework to test, not gospel
modules.in_your_system: This matters more for you than for a discretionary trader: a bot forces every 'sufficient displacement?' judgment into explicit, backtestable rules. Your backtest sweeps and shadow soaks are precisely where these critiques get tested against reality.

resources.title: Is ICT Trading Legit? The Truth About Profitability
resources.note: Explains the concepts fairly while separating verified edge from ICT's 70–80% win-rate claims.

resources.title: ICT SMC Entry Models: A Critical Guide
resources.note: Names the falsifiability problem head-on — subjective inputs resist systematic backtesting. Essential before you trust bot rules built on them.

resources.title: Understanding SMC — Core Principles (skeptic's view)
resources.note: Lays out the main criticisms: whether institutions use ICT, whether retail liquidity is worth 'hunting,' and possible repackaging of classic price action.

resources.title: BabyPips: 'ICT and order blocks — anybody use this?'
resources.note: Real practitioners debating what works vs. hype — a good sanity-check on marketing claims.

tracks.name: The Machines
tracks.blurb: AI judgment from zero. We start with what machine learning actually is, build the vocabulary to make strategic calls, then land on the exact pieces already running in your bot — and on directing Claude to do the heavy technical lifting for you.

modules.title: AI/ML Mental Models
modules.goal: The absolute basics, no math or code. What ML is, how a model 'learns,' and what a neural network really does.
goals: Explain supervised vs. unsupervised learning in one sentence each
Build an intuition for how a model 'learns' from examples
Right-size your expectations: models are powerful pattern-matchers, not minds

resources.title: Elements of AI — Introduction to AI
resources.by: University of Helsinki + Reaktor · ~30 hrs, no code
resources.note: The best beginner course going: what AI is, what it can and can't do, and how models learn — zero math or programming.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Neural Networks
resources.by: Grant Sanderson · watch for the ideas, not the equations
resources.note: The most beautiful visual explanation of what a neural net is and how it learns. Start with 'But what is a Neural Network?'

resources.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
resources.by: Google for Developers · concept-only front door
resources.note: Short plain-language primer on supervised vs. unsupervised learning and the core ML vocabulary.

resources.title: You Look Like a Thing and I Love You
resources.note: A funny, cartoon-illustrated tour of how ML actually behaves — 'more like a toaster than Skynet.' The most painless on-ramp here.

modules.title: The ML Lifecycle
modules.goal: The end-to-end loop a model lives in: data → features → training → evaluation → deployment → monitoring.
goals: Name the stages of building and running an ML system
Know where a decision-maker should ask hard questions at each stage
See ML as an iterative loop, not a one-time build
modules.in_your_system: This is literally your trainer pipeline: dataset builds → training cycles → the model registry → shadow soak → promotion. Recognizing the generic lifecycle makes your own trainer VM and /ml-review legible.

resources.title: The Machine Learning Life Cycle Explained
resources.note: Clear walkthrough of the full loop framed as an iterative process — the cleanest single overview.

resources.title: Explaining the ML Life Cycle to Business Execs
resources.by: InfoWorld · written for your exact seat
resources.note: Analogies over jargon; stresses where leaders should ask questions rather than sweat technical detail.

resources.title: People + AI Guidebook
resources.note: Action-oriented patterns for overseeing AI products — defining success, evaluation, feedback loops, graceful failure. Judgment, not code.

modules.title: Evaluating Models & Avoiding Traps
modules.goal: The single most important strategic skill: knowing when a good-looking result is lying to you.
goals: Read accuracy, precision, and recall — and know why 'accuracy' misleads on rare events
Understand train/validation/test splits and why a great score on the wrong data means nothing
Spot overfitting and data leakage — the two ways a model fools its builder
modules.in_your_system: This is the judgment behind every promotion decision — your shadow models earn a track record before they're allowed to influence a live order precisely because a backtest score alone can't be trusted. Pair with Modules 15–17.

resources.title: Accuracy, Recall, Precision & Related Metrics
resources.note: The cleanest explainer of the confusion matrix and why accuracy misleads on imbalanced problems — the base-rate trap.

resources.title: Train, Validation, Test Split
resources.note: Why data is split three ways, and how a leaked test set produces a model that aces the exam and fails in the real world — the core of 'a great backtest can lie.'

resources.title: Train/Validation/Test: How-To & Best Practices
resources.note: Reinforces overfitting with the 'student who memorized past exam papers' analogy, plus data-leakage pitfalls in plain language.

modules.title: AI Strategy & Judgment for Leaders
modules.goal: When ML is the right tool, when it isn't, and how to think about it as an investment rather than a gadget.
goals: Judge which problems ML genuinely fits — and which it doesn't
Reframe AI as 'cheap prediction' and reason about where its value actually lands
Learn from famous AI failures so you don't repeat them

resources.title: AI for Everyone
resources.by: Andrew Ng / DeepLearning.AI · free to audit, no code
resources.note: The definitive non-technical leadership course: what AI realistically can/can't do, spotting good use cases, working with an AI team, building a strategy. If you do one thing in this track, do this.

resources.title: Prediction Machines
resources.note: Reframes AI as 'cheap prediction,' giving you a real economic framework for when ML helps and where its value actually shows up.

resources.title: How to Choose Your First AI Project
resources.note: A practical where-to-start guide: pick fast, high-odds, domain-specific pilots that build buy-in.

resources.title: 5 Big AI Failures That Show What Can Go Wrong
resources.note: Digestible case studies (biased hiring models, Zillow's $500M loss) with the human-in-the-loop lessons a leader should internalize.

modules.title: LLM & Generative-AI Literacy
modules.goal: How large language models — the thing you're talking to right now — actually work, and where they fail.
goals: Understand at a concept level how an LLM generates text
Know why they 'hallucinate' and what that means for trusting output
Match the tool to the job: what LLMs are genuinely good and bad at

resources.title: Generative AI Exists Because of the Transformer
resources.note: The single best scroll-through explanation of how LLMs generate text and why they hallucinate — no background needed.

resources.title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models
resources.by: Andrej Karpathy · a leading AI researcher, general audience
resources.note: What LLMs are, how they're trained, their strengths, limits, and security concerns like prompt injection. Deep but accessible.

resources.title: Why Large Language Models Hallucinate
resources.note: Why LLMs confidently invent things — they predict text, they don't look up facts — and how a user can reduce it.

resources.title: What Are Large Language Models?
resources.note: A reputable reference on how LLMs work conceptually and where they fit. Good to bookmark.

modules.title: Working Effectively With Claude
modules.goal: Your force multiplier. You don't need to code — you need to direct, delegate, and verify. This module is how.
goals: Know what to delegate to Claude vs. what to decide yourself (the human-in-the-loop line)
Write clear instructions: name the task, say who the output is for, define 'done,' give examples
Evaluate output you can't read line-by-line — by asking for explanations, checks, and evidence
modules.in_your_system: This is exactly how you already operate your bot: Claude pulls VM state, runs backtests, writes code, and proposes changes; you approve the tier-gated calls and set direction. This module sharpens that division of labor — the better you brief and verify, the more you can safely hand off.

resources.title: Co-Intelligence: Living and Working with AI
resources.by: Ethan Mollick (Wharton) · for a general audience
resources.note: The best non-technical playbook for treating AI as a collaborator: what to delegate, how to stay human-in-the-loop, how to judge output.

resources.title: One Useful Thing
resources.note: Practical prompting and AI-use advice. Core lesson: talk to it like a person, and learn by ~10 hours of hands-on use.

resources.title: Prompt Engineering Overview (Claude)
resources.note: The source-of-truth on directing Claude well: be explicit about the task, the audience, and what 'done' looks like — and give examples.

resources.title: Interactive Prompt Engineering Tutorial
resources.note: Work through it to build real judgment about steering and checking an AI partner — a non-coder can absolutely do this.

modules.title: ML in Trading — The Hard Truths
modules.goal: Where the two tracks meet. Markets are a uniquely brutal place for ML — understand why before you trust a model with money.
goals: Understand why markets defeat naive ML: low signal-to-noise, non-stationarity, adaptive opponents
Calibrate expectations — separate genuine edge from 'AI will print money' hype
Know the specific failure modes that sink ML trading funds
modules.in_your_system: This is the reality check behind your conservative, shadow-first ML design. Your models log predictions for a long soak and are graded before promotion because of everything in this module.

resources.title: The 10 Reasons Most Machine Learning Funds Fail
resources.note: The single best strategic read on why ML in markets is uniquely hard — from the field's most cited skeptic-practitioner.

resources.title: Benefits, Pitfalls & Mitigation of ML in Trading
resources.note: A balanced, jargon-light survey of what ML can and can't do for trading. Good hype-detox.

resources.title: ML for Trading — From Idea to Execution
resources.by: Stefan Jansen · read the concepts, skip the code
resources.note: Plain-language framing of where ML actually fits in a trading workflow.

resources.title: López de Prado — Free Lecture Slides
resources.note: The conceptual slides behind the canonical book; skim the diagrams and section headers for the strategic ideas.

modules.title: Backtesting Done Right
modules.goal: Where most bad decisions get made. A backtest is trivially easy to make look great — and usually is.
goals: Understand walk-forward analysis (rolling out-of-sample testing) and why it's the honest way
Recognize backtest overfitting, survivorship bias, and look-ahead bias
Ask the right questions of any backtest before believing it
modules.in_your_system: Your trainer VM runs backtest sweeps and your harness does walk-forward validation before any Tier-3 change ships. This module is the theory those guardrails exist to honor.

resources.title: How Backtest Overfitting Leads to False Discoveries
resources.by: Bailey & López de Prado · Mathematical Investor
resources.note: The accessible version of 'you can always find a backtest that looks great' — the mental model to internalize above all others here.

resources.title: Walk-Forward Analysis Explained
resources.note: A clear walk-through of rolling in-sample / out-of-sample testing — directly relevant to your bot's validation.

resources.title: Survivorship Bias in Backtesting
resources.note: How survivorship and related biases silently inflate backtest results.

resources.title: Pseudo-Mathematics and Financial Charlatanism
resources.by: Bailey, Borwein, López de Prado & Zhu · read the abstract + intro
resources.note: The famous proof that impressive backtests are trivially easy to manufacture. Skim it — the message is what matters.

modules.title: Safe, Staged ML Deployment
modules.goal: How to put a model into production without letting it hurt you — the discipline your bot already embodies.
goals: Understand shadow deployment: run a model live but log-only before it can act
Grasp champion-challenger promotion — when a new model earns the right to take over
Know what model/data drift is and when to promote, retrain, or kill a model
modules.in_your_system: This maps exactly onto your 3-stage ladder — candidate → shadow → advisory. Shadow models observe and log; only 'advisory' influences an order; the shadow→advisory step is the operator-gated promotion gate. This module is the industry-standard framing of your own design.

resources.title: Shadow Deployment for ML Models
resources.note: Run a new model on live data, log-only, before it can touch decisions — and the promote/kill logic. This IS your shadow stage.

resources.title: Champion-Challenger Model
resources.note: The promotion framework in plain terms — when a challenger earns the right to replace the incumbent.

resources.title: Monitoring & Feedback in the AI Lifecycle
resources.note: Production monitoring, data/model drift, and retraining triggers — the 'how do I know when to act' half.

resources.title: Canary & Shadow Testing Patterns
resources.note: Compares staged-rollout patterns (shadow → canary → A/B) so you can reason about how aggressively to promote.

modules.title: Features, Targets & Data Leakage
modules.goal: What a model actually learns from, what it's asked to predict, and the invisible bug that fakes brilliance: seeing the future.
goals: Understand 'features' (the inputs) and 'targets/labels' (what the model predicts) in a trading context
Grasp look-ahead bias / data leakage — the #1 way time-series ML cheats
See how trading ML defines smarter targets than 'will price go up?'
modules.in_your_system: Your bot engineers features from market data and cross-asset peers, and captures decision-time snapshots so training reflects only what was knowable then. That care exists to prevent exactly the leakage this module explains.

resources.title: Look-Ahead Bias: The Invisible Killer
resources.note: The most common way time-series ML 'cheats' by peeking at the future — the failure mode to fear most in your system.

resources.title: Triple-Barrier Labelling
resources.note: How trading ML defines targets (take-profit / stop-loss / time-out) instead of naive 'price up tomorrow' — the López de Prado labeling idea, made accessible.

resources.title: Understanding & Avoiding Look-Ahead Bias
resources.note: A second, practitioner-angle take on data leakage — reinforces the concept with concrete examples.

resources.title: Advances in Financial ML — Chapter 1
resources.note: Covers why financial data structures, labeling, and sample weighting are different. Dip in for the concepts.

modules.title: The Quant Big Picture + Staying Current
modules.goal: Zoom out to systematic trading as a whole, and set up the feeds that keep you learning after this curriculum.
goals: Understand edge, edge decay, and combining 'good enough' strategies
See the breadth of researched strategies to calibrate what 'edge' looks like
Pick a couple of ongoing sources to follow the field over time

resources.title: QuantStart — Systematic Trading
resources.note: Broad coverage of strategy design, risk, and the systematic-trading mindset.

resources.title: Robot Wealth
resources.note: The best accessible writing on edge, expectancy, edge decay, and combining strategies — highly relevant to your strategic calls.

resources.title: Flirting with Models
resources.note: The premier podcast on how quant/systematic strategies are actually researched and run.

resources.title: Quantocracy
resources.by: daily curated feed · + Quantpedia strategy encyclopedia
resources.note: One bookmark to follow the whole field — the best quant/algo blog posts, curated daily.

resources.title: Ernest Chan — Quantitative Trading (+ shelf references)
resources.by: + Advances in Financial ML (López de Prado) & Jansen's ML for Trading (free code repo)
resources.note: The canonical shelf: Chan for a gentle systematic-trading on-ramp; the other two as aspirational references you own and dip into, not read cover-to-cover.

quickstart.title: If you only do five things
steps: Elements of AI (M8) + AI for Everyone (M11) — your AI foundation, no code.
BabyPips School of Pipsology, early grades (M1) — trading from zero.
The 13-minute ICT overview then the 2022 mentorship playlist (M3) — your bot's native language.
Backtest overfitting + walk-forward analysis (M15) — the judgment that protects your capital.
Shadow deployment (M16) + Co-Intelligence (M13) — understand your ML pipeline and get more from Claude.

cadence.title: A rough 8-week cadence
steps: Weeks 1–2 · Foundations, both sides. Markets: Modules 1–2. Machines: Modules 8–9. Goal: read a chart and explain what ML is.
Weeks 3–4 · Your system's language. Markets: Modules 3–4 (ICT + your setups), reading Module 7's critiques alongside. Machines: Modules 10 + 12.
Weeks 5–6 · The judgment layer. Markets: Modules 5–6 (risk + psychology). Machines: Modules 11 + 13 (AI strategy + working with Claude).
Weeks 7–8 · Where they meet. Machines: Modules 14–17 (ML in trading, backtesting, deployment, leakage) — the modules that map straight onto your bot. Then Module 18 to set up ongoing feeds.

scam_flags.title: Scam flags — what to avoid
items: Anyone promising fixed returns, 'signals,' 'funded challenge' upsells, or profit screenshots — the classic get-rich-quick pattern.
Paid 'ICT mentorships' and Discord signal groups — the real ICT material is 100% free on the official channel.
Lookalike scraper domains impersonating trusted brands (e.g. marketinvestopedia.com) — cite only the real investopedia.com / stockcharts.com.
'AI trading bot will make you rich' content — you already know from Module 14 why that's the tell.
